narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam. This model assesses the likelihood of passing the bar on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and other background factors, and unfortunately, it predicted an 80.5% chance that you would fail— a threshold that led to the rejection of your application to protect the university's outcomes. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, where you placed in the bottom 10% of your peers, strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar exam struggles compared to the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your Year 1 decile was similarly in the lowest 10%, which reinforced this concern and was the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, far below the average student's standing. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 sat a bit under the typical 3.21, it actually worked slightly in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting success rather than the opposite. Your LSAT score of 31 and choice to pursue full-time studies also provided small positive influences, countering the risk just a little against the dataset norms where scores average 36.7 and most students go full-time anyway. Overall, your profile stood out negatively because those early and later low decile rankings deviated sharply from what the model sees in students who typically pass, overshadowing your other attributes. We understand this is disappointing, especially given your solid family support and dedication to full-time effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and focusing on improving your class rankings in any future studies. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these core academic gaps—reach out to admissions advisors for targeted advice, and reapply once you've bolstered these areas. Your path to law school is still very much possible with these steps. We're rooting for you.
